learning_objectives: Define risk, threat, vulnerability, and their relationships
Compare major risk management frameworks (NIST RMF, ISO 27005, FAIR)
readings: ISO 27005:2022 Information Security Risk Management overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Risk management transforms cybersecurity from a technical discipline into a business enabler. It provides the language for communicating security investment to executives.
key_concepts: Risk equation: Risk = Threat x Vulnerability x Impact
NIST RMF: Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor
ISO 27005: Context, Risk Assessment, Risk Treatment, Monitoring
FAIR model: quantitative risk analysis with financial outcomes
Risk appetite, tolerance, and acceptance criteria
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing how NIST RMF and FAIR would assess the same risk scenario: a vulnerable public-facing web application

review.value: Six-step framework: Categorize system, Select controls, Implement controls, Assess effectiveness, Authorize operation, Monitor continuously. Mandatory for U.S. federal systems.
Factor Analysis of Information Risk: quantitative model that estimates risk in financial terms. Breaks risk into Loss Event Frequency and Loss Magnitude with defined factors.

learning_objectives: Apply STRIDE and PASTA threat modeling methodologies
Conduct vulnerability assessments using automated and manual techniques
Prioritize vulnerabilities using CVSS and risk context
readings: Threat Modeling: Designing for Security Ch. 1-4
NIST SP 800-30 Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments

lecture_notes.introduction: Threat modeling systematically identifies what can go wrong, while vulnerability assessment finds where the weaknesses actually are. Together they form the analytical core of risk assessment.
key_concepts: STRIDE: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege
PASTA: Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis
Data flow diagrams and trust boundaries
CVSS v3.1 scoring: Base, Temporal, Environmental metrics
Vulnerability prioritization with business context
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a STRIDE threat model for a web application with a data flow diagram, then mapping identified threats to discovered vulnerabilities
setup_requirements: Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool or OWASP Threat Dragon

review.value: Threat categorization model mapping to security properties: Spoofing (Authentication), Tampering (Integrity), Repudiation (Non-repudiation), Info Disclosure (Confidentiality), DoS (Availability), EoP (Authorization)
Adjusts the base score based on organization-specific factors: how critical the affected asset is, what compensating controls exist, and whether the vulnerability is reachable

learning_objectives: Perform quantitative risk analysis using ALE methodology
Conduct qualitative risk analysis with risk matrices
readings: CISSP Study Guide: Risk Analysis chapter

lecture_notes.introduction: Risk analysis translates technical findings into business terms. Quantitative analysis provides dollar figures; qualitative analysis provides relative prioritization. Both serve different audiences.
key_concepts: Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) = SLE x ARO
Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) = Asset Value x Exposure Factor
Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) estimation
Qualitative risk matrices: likelihood vs impact
Return on Security Investment: ROSI = (ALE_before - ALE_after - Cost) / Cost
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating ALE for a data breach scenario, comparing control options by ROSI, and presenting the business case to simulated executives

review.value: Example: Database with $2M records, 40% exposure factor = $800K SLE. Estimated 0.1 ARO = $80K ALE. A $50K/year control reducing ARO to 0.01 yields ROSI of ($80K - $8K - $50K)/$50K = 44%.

Qualitative is faster and easier but subjective. Quantitative is more rigorous but requires data that may be estimated. Best practice uses both: qualitative for prioritization, quantitative for investment decisions.

learning_objectives: Evaluate risk treatment options: mitigate, transfer, accept, avoid
Select appropriate controls from NIST SP 800-53
Design a risk treatment plan with compensating controls
readings: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 Security and Privacy Controls

lecture_notes.introduction: Risk treatment is where assessment meets action. The goal is to reduce risk to an acceptable level through the most cost-effective combination of controls.
key_concepts: Risk treatment options: mitigate, transfer (insurance), accept, avoid
NIST SP 800-53 control families and selection process
Compensating controls when primary controls are infeasible
Control layering and defense in depth
Residual risk documentation and acceptance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Selecting and mapping NIST SP 800-53 controls to identified risks from the threat model, calculating residual risk after control implementation

review.value: Transferring financial impact to a third party, typically through cyber insurance. Does not eliminate the risk; only shifts the financial burden. The operational and reputational impact remains.
Alternative controls that provide equivalent protection when a recommended control cannot be implemented. Must be documented and justified in the risk assessment.

class_schedule.topic: Third-Party and Supply Chain Risk Management
learning_objectives: Assess cybersecurity risks from third-party vendors
Design a vendor risk assessment program
readings: NIST SP 800-161 Supply Chain Risk Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizations are only as secure as their weakest vendor. Major breaches increasingly originate through third-party access, making supply chain risk management essential.
key_concepts: Third-party risk categories: operational, compliance, strategic, reputational
Vendor risk assessment: SIG questionnaires, SOC 2 reports, penetration test results
Software supply chain: SBOM, dependency scanning, build integrity
Vendor tiering: critical, high, medium, low based on data access
Continuous monitoring of third-party risk posture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a vendor risk assessment using the SIG Lite questionnaire and analyzing a SOC 2 Type II report for control effectiveness

review.value: Software Bill of Materials: a complete inventory of all components in a software product. Enables vulnerability tracking when a library vulnerability is disclosed (e.g., Log4Shell).
Third-party audit of a service organization's controls over a period (typically 12 months). Type II is more valuable than Type I because it tests control effectiveness over time, not just design.

learning_objectives: Implement a continuous risk monitoring program
Develop risk dashboards and executive reports
Integrate risk management with security operations

lecture_notes.introduction: Risk management is not a point-in-time activity. Continuous monitoring ensures that risk assessments remain current as threats, vulnerabilities, and business contexts evolve.
key_concepts: Continuous monitoring: automated vulnerability scanning, configuration auditing
Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) and Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)
Risk dashboards for executive communication
Integration with GRC platforms
Risk reporting frequency and audience adaptation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a risk dashboard that aggregates vulnerability scan data, compliance status, and incident metrics into executive-level risk reporting

review.value: KRIs are leading indicators of increasing risk (e.g., unpatched critical vulnerabilities count). KPIs measure control effectiveness (e.g., percentage of systems patched within SLA).
Translate technical risk into business terms: financial exposure, regulatory compliance status, trend analysis. Executives need to make investment decisions, not understand technical details.

quizzes.topic: Risk Frameworks, Threat Modeling, and Risk Analysis

questions.question: A hospital's patient portal application stores PHI (Protected Health Information). Using STRIDE, identify one specific threat for each of the six categories that applies to this system, and for each threat, name the corresponding security property at risk.
questions.answer: Spoofing (Authentication): Attacker uses stolen credentials to access another patient's records. Tampering (Integrity): Attacker modifies prescription data in transit between browser and server. Repudiation (Non-repudiation): User denies viewing specific records; system has no audit trail. Information Disclosure (Confidentiality): SQL injection exposes the entire patient database. Denial of Service (Availability): Attacker floods the portal with requests, preventing patients from accessing their records. Elevation of Privilege (Authorization): Patient exploits an IDOR vulnerability to access administrative functions.

rubric.key: Threats are specific and realistic for a healthcare portal
Correct security property mapped to each threat

questions.question: A company's e-commerce platform generates $10M annually. A risk assessment identifies that a DDoS attack could take the platform offline. Historical data suggests one significant DDoS attack every 2 years, with average downtime of 4 hours. Calculate the ALE and determine if a $75K/year DDoS mitigation service (reducing ARO to once every 10 years and downtime to 15 minutes) is justified using ROSI.
questions.answer: Current: Hourly revenue = $10M / 8760 hours = $1,142/hour. SLE = $1,142 x 4 hours = $4,568. ARO = 0.5 (once per 2 years). ALE_before = $4,568 x 0.5 = $2,284. With mitigation: SLE_new = $1,142 x 0.25 hours = $286. ARO_new = 0.1. ALE_after = $286 x 0.1 = $29. ROSI = ($2,284 - $29 - $75,000) / $75,000 = -97%. The mitigation service is NOT justified on a pure ALE basis; the cost ($75K) far exceeds the expected annual loss ($2,284). However, this narrow calculation ignores reputational damage, customer churn, and regulatory penalties which could increase the SLE by orders of magnitude.

rubric.key: Recognizes limitations of narrow ALE analysis

quizzes.topic: Risk Treatment, Supply Chain, and Monitoring

questions.question: Your organization uses a critical SaaS payroll provider. The vendor's SOC 2 Type II report reveals two exceptions: failed MFA enforcement for admin accounts and backup encryption gaps. As the risk manager, what actions would you take and what contractual remedies would you pursue?
questions.answer: Immediate actions: (1) Assess severity: MFA failure on admin accounts is critical (direct access to all payroll data), backup encryption gap is high (data exposure if backup media is compromised). (2) Request a formal remediation plan with specific timeline from the vendor (30 days for MFA, 60 days for backup encryption). (3) Escalate to the vendor's account executive and reference SLA/contract security requirements. Contractual remedies: (4) Issue a formal risk acceptance deviation if the vendor cannot remediate within the timeline, documenting compensating controls. (5) Invoke the right-to-audit clause for independent verification of remediation. (6) If unresolved, invoke the contract's security breach clause and begin evaluating alternative vendors. (7) Increase monitoring: request monthly status updates and evidence of MFA enforcement logs.

questions.question: Design three Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) for monitoring cybersecurity risk at the executive level. For each KRI, specify: what it measures, the data source, the threshold that triggers escalation, and what executive action it should prompt.
questions.answer: KRI 1: 'Critical unpatched vulnerability age' - Measures average days critical (CVSS 9+) vulnerabilities remain unpatched. Data source: vulnerability scanner (Qualys/Tenable). Threshold: >14 days triggers escalation. Executive action: authorize emergency patching resources or accept risk formally. KRI 2: 'Phishing click rate' - Measures percentage of employees clicking phishing simulation links. Data source: security awareness platform. Threshold: >5% triggers escalation. Executive action: mandate additional training or invest in email security controls. KRI 3: 'Third-party critical risk count' - Measures number of critical vendors with risk scores below acceptable threshold. Data source: vendor risk management platform. Threshold: >2 critical vendors below threshold triggers escalation. Executive action: initiate vendor remediation or approve vendor migration budget.

major_assignments.title: Enterprise Risk Assessment
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive cybersecurity risk assessment for a provided fictional organization including threat modeling, vulnerability analysis, quantitative risk calculations, and a risk treatment plan.
objectives: Apply NIST RMF and FAIR methodologies to a realistic scenario
Calculate ALE and ROSI for identified risks
Develop a prioritized risk treatment plan
deliverables: Risk assessment report with threat model and vulnerability analysis
Quantitative risk analysis with ALE and ROSI calculations
Risk treatment plan mapping controls to identified risks
Executive risk summary with dashboard visualizations

criteria: All critical assets identified and valued
Correct ALE calculations with justified assumptions
Executive summary accessible to non-technical leadership
Thorough risk identification covering all critical assets and threat vectors
Rigorous quantitative analysis with defensible assumptions
Practical risk treatment plan aligned with business objectives

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of risk management concepts, frameworks, and calculations

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises with threat modeling, risk analysis, and vendor assessment

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering risk frameworks, threat modeling, and quantitative analysis

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all risk assessment and management topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Case study discussions may be collaborative. All risk assessments and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All analysis must be original. Risk calculations must show work. Copying frameworks without analysis is not acceptable.
course_policies.professional_conduct: Students are expected to maintain confidentiality of case study details as practice for handling sensitive organizational risk information.
